summary: Jeff Atwood traces his history with Alan Cooper's 'About Face' book across its multiple editions from 1995 to 2007. He notes how each edition improved upon the last, with additional co-authors diluting Cooper's natural bombast while adding broader perspectives. The third edition features better organization, more detailed coverage of Goal-Directed Design, and improved visual design concepts. Atwood enthusiastically recommends the latest edition as the definitive version of this interaction design classic.
key_points: Each new edition of About Face obsoletes the previous one, with the third being definitively the best
Adding co-authors improved the book by balancing Cooper's bombastic tendencies with broader perspectives
The 2007 edition reorganizes content into three parts: process/users, interaction principles, and interface principles
Design books face the same obsolescence cycle as software itself
Goal-Directed Design process coverage was significantly expanded in the third edition
money_quotes: Seems quaint now, if not borderline obsolete, but this was 12 whole years ago. That's almost a lifetime in computer dog-years.
I love Cooper, but he can be awfully bombastic at times. Having a second author dilutes Cooper's natural bombast and adds another viewpoint for a broader perspective.
Cooper is obsoleting his own books at a frantic pace; it's almost as bad as software.
You're about to read one of the best books ever written on interaction design. Enjoy.
I envy the experience you're about to have. For the rest of us, time to pony up the upgrade fee. Again.
key_insight: Classic technical books require continuous revision to remain relevant, and each iteration can genuinely improve upon the last through additional perspectives and updated practices.
